sections.title: Mexico Real Estate
sections.contentHtml:
Feeds Compliance Policy 2026

Expert Edition for Real Estate Marketplace Platforms

Verified & Updated — March 16, 2026

DOCUMENT PURPOSE: This document is the authoritative compliance reference for real estate marketplace platforms operating in Mexico as of March 2026. It covers market structure, AMPI and MLS systems, portal feeds, NOM-247 consumer standards, AML (LFPIORPI 2025 reform), foreign ownership rules, taxes, and data privacy.

CRITICAL STRUCTURAL DIFFERENCE: Mexico has NO national MLS. There is no federal licensing requirement for real estate agents. AMPI membership is voluntary. Listing data flows through competing commercial portals, not a regulated central database. Some resort and coastal markets have regional AMPI-affiliated MLS systems.

sections.title: Market Structure — No National MLS
sections.contentHtml:
Mexico's real estate market operates without a national Multiple Listing Service or any equivalent centralized database. Unlike the United States, there is no federal IDX, VOW, or RESO-standard data feed framework. Agents, developers, and private sellers distribute listings independently across a competitive ecosystem of commercial portals. In certain high-activity tourist and coastal markets, regional AMPI chapter-affiliated MLS systems operate locally — but these are optional, not nationally mandated.

A further critical distinction: there is no government-issued real estate agent license in Mexico at the federal level. Anybody can legally offer properties for sale without certification. AMPI membership, while widely respected and the industry's best-practice standard, is voluntary.

TopicUSA (MLS-based)
Portugal (Portal-based)Mexico (Portal + Regional MLS)
National MLSYES — regional per area
NONO — regional only in select markets
Federal agent licensingYES — state-issued
YES — IMPIC/AMINO — voluntary AMPI only
Mandatory data sharingYES — IDX/VOW rules
NONO
Feed standardRESO Web API
NoneNone — each portal proprietary
Consumer protection NOMNo direct equivalent
GDPR / CNPDNOM-247-SE-2021 (in force Sept 2022)
Foreign buyer restrictionNone
None (EU rules)YES — Restricted Zone + Fideicomiso
AML obligationSAR filing (FinCEN)
GDPR / national AMLLFPIORPI (July 2025 reform)

________________

sections.title: Regulatory Bodies
sections.contentHtml:
PROFECO — Consumer Protection Authority

The Procuraduría Federal del Consumidor (PROFECO) is Mexico's federal consumer protection agency and the primary regulator of real estate advertising, contracts, and commercial practices. PROFECO enforces NOM-247-SE-2021 and the Federal Consumer Protection Law (LFPC). Marketplace platforms and agents marketing residential properties are directly subject to PROFECO oversight. Consumers may file complaints directly with PROFECO for conciliation, and PROFECO can impose fines and suspend activities for non-compliance.

* Official website: profeco.gob.mx

* Maximum fine for NOM-247 non-compliance: MXN $5,116,688 (five million pesos — as set in NOM-247)

* Governs: advertising, contract registration, consumer rights, disclosure, and complaint resolution

AMPI — Professional Association

The Asociación Mexicana de Profesionales Inmobiliarios (AMPI) is Mexico's national real estate professional association, established in 1956. AMPI is the Mexican equivalent of the U.S. NAR — it has signed a formal joint-venture with NAR, such that AMPI members automatically receive NAR membership. AMPI sets ethical standards, provides education, and operates regional MLS systems in select markets. Crucially, AMPI membership is entirely voluntary.

* Website: ampi.org

* Membership: approximately 5,000 members in 22+ cities and an international section

* Requirements: 100 hours of continuing education and testing, verifiable experience, community involvement

* Code of ethics: AMPI members are bound by fiduciary and ethical standards, though enforcement is association-level only

* NAR relationship: AMPI-NAR joint venture — AMPI membership confers NAR membership

* AMPI is NOT a licensing body — it cannot issue or revoke government-recognized credentials

FOR PLATFORMS: Because there is no mandatory licensing, the only meaningful credential check a platform can perform is (1) AMPI membership status, (2) NOM-247 contract registration with PROFECO, and (3) RFC/tax registration with SAT.

SAT — Tax Authority

The Servicio de Administración Tributaria (SAT) administers all federal taxes, including ISR (income tax on capital gains and rental income) and IVA (VAT). SAT also supervises compliance with LFPIORPI's vulnerable activities reporting requirements for real estate transactions. All parties to a property transaction require an RFC (Registro Federal de Contribuyentes — tax identification number) from SAT.

* Portal: sat.gob.mx

* AML supervision: SAT oversees LFPIORPI compliance for non-financial obligated entities including real estate

SHCP — Ministry of Finance (LFPIORPI Supervisor)

The Secretaría de Hacienda y Crédito Público (SHCP) is the primary authority for enforcing the LFPIORPI anti-money laundering law. Real estate transactions are classified as 'vulnerable activities' under LFPIORPI and are subject to SHCP oversight via its Financial Intelligence Unit (Unidad de Inteligencia Financiera — UIF). The July 2025 LFPIORPI reform significantly expanded SHCP's enforcement powers.

SRE — Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Foreign Buyer Permits)

The Secretaría de Relaciones Exteriores (SRE) issues the government permit required for foreigners to establish a fideicomiso (bank trust) to purchase property in Mexico's Restricted Zone. SRE permit approval typically takes five business days.

INFONAVIT / FOVISSSTE — Housing Finance

INFONAVIT (Instituto del Fondo Nacional de la Vivienda para los Trabajadores) and FOVISSSTE (Fondo de la Vivienda del ISSSTE) are Mexico's primary social housing finance institutions, providing mortgage credit to formal-sector workers. Many residential listings at the entry and mid-market levels qualify for INFONAVIT-backed credit — this affects listing data requirements for platforms that integrate mortgage pre-qualification tools.

sections.title: AMPI Regional MLS Systems
sections.contentHtml:
In select tourist and coastal markets, AMPI chapters operate local MLS systems. These are voluntary for AMPI members, not nationally mandated. There is no RESO standard, no OData API, and no IDX/VOW framework — each local MLS operates its own database and rules. Platforms wishing to access MLS data in these markets must execute separate agreements with each regional MLS.

MLS NameRegion
Key PlatformNotes
Vallarta-Nayarit MLSPuerto Vallarta / Riviera Nayarit
MLSVallarta.com + VallartaNayaritMLS.comOldest MLS in Mexico (operating since 1988–1989). AMPI Vallarta, AMPI Riviera Nayarit, AMPI Compostela affiliated. Uses FlexMLS platform. Pre-construction MLS rules apply.
Los Cabos MLSBaja California Sur (Cabo San Lucas / San José del Cabo)
Los Cabos MLS platformCovers one of Mexico's highest-value foreign buyer markets. AMPI Los Cabos affiliated. Fideicomiso zone — all residential in restricted zone.
Riviera Maya / Cancún MLSQuintana Roo (Cancún, Playa del Carmen, Tulum)
Various AMPI Q. Roo chaptersHigh foreign buyer volume. Restricted zone — fideicomiso required. Multiple AMPI chapters with overlapping coverage.
Mexico City (CDMX)Mexico City
No formal MLS — portal-drivenLargest market by volume. No AMPI MLS. Listings distributed via Inmuebles24, Propiedades.com, etc. Outside restricted zone — direct title possible for all buyers.
Guadalajara / JaliscoJalisco
AMPI Guadalajara (no centralized MLS)Second-largest city. Portal-driven market. AMPI Guadalajara active but no regional MLS platform.
Monterrey / Nuevo LeónNuevo León
AMPI Monterrey (no centralized MLS)Major industrial and business market. Outside restricted zone. No formal MLS.

KEY RULE — Pre-Construction MLS Entry (Vallarta-Nayarit): Within 7 days of a ratified Purchase Agreement, the pre-construction/new construction listing must be entered into the MLS. The developer must demonstrate financial capability to complete construction. The lot owner must have executed a listing agreement with an MLS participant. If all lots under a blanket agreement will not be entered within 3 days of ratification, the agreement is submitted as an Office Exclusive.

NOM-247 PROFECO CONTRACT GATE — AMPI MLS Enforcement (Previously Missing): AMPI chapters in the Vallarta-Nayarit MLS now require agents to upload a valid PROFECO adhesion contract registration number before any listing can be entered into the MLS system. Listings submitted without a confirmed PROFECO contract number are rejected at the MLS entry stage. Platforms ingesting data from these regional MLS systems will not receive listings from non-compliant agents — building in a natural PROFECO compliance filter. This enforcement pattern is expanding to other AMPI chapters.

sections.title: Listing Portal Landscape
sections.contentHtml:
Mexico's listing market is dominated by a small number of large portals, with a significant ownership consolidation having occurred in 2022 when Brazilian PropTech giant QuintoAndar acquired both Inmuebles24 (the market leader) and Vivanuncios. This duopoly structure has significant implications for marketplace platforms seeking feed integrations.

Major Portals — Reference Table

PortalURL
OwnerPosition
Key NotesInmuebles24
inmuebles24.comQuintoAndar (Brazil)
#1Market leader. Over 424,000 active listings. QuintoAndar also offers free CRM to agents — used to discourage third-party CRM integrations.
Propiedades.compropiedades.com
Independent (acquired 2022)#2
Claims 1.2M active listings, 270,000 exclusives. Strong in mid-market residential. Rose to #2 after QuintoAndar consolidation.Vivanuncios
vivanuncios.com.mxQuintoAndar (Brazil)
#3Historically #2; dropped after QuintoAndar shifted focus to Inmuebles24 brand. Stronger in secondary cities.
Lamudi Méxicolamudi.com.mx
Lifull (Japan, acquired 2023 from Dubizzle)#4
New-build and premium residential focus. Also integrated into Proppit aggregator network (Trovit, Mitula, Icasas, Wasi).Metros Cúbicos
metroscubicos.comMercadoLibre
#5MercadoLibre marketplace integration gives high visibility. Mixed residential and commercial.
MercadoLibre Inmueblesinmuebles.mercadolibre.com.mx
MercadoLibre#5 (same)
MercadoLibre's native real estate section. Very high traffic from MercadoLibre's general marketplace.Trovit / Mitula / Icasas
trovit.com.mx / icasas.mxLifull / Proppit
AggregatorAggregate listings from multiple sources. Part of Lifull/Proppit network. Good for reach; low lead quality.
Clau.comclau.com
Mexican PropTech startupChallenger
Super-app model: listings + mortgage + AVM. Data analytics focus. Growing in CDMX.Doorvel
doorvel.comIndependent
NicheNewer platform, lightweight UX, personalised recommendations.
REMAX Méxicoremax.com.mx
REMAX (franchise)Agency brand
REMAX franchise network brand site. Not a general aggregator.Century 21 México
century21mexico.comCentury 21 (franchise)
Agency brandCentury 21 franchise brand site.

Portal Compliance Obligations

* NOM-247-SE-2021 applies to all advertising on all portals — truthful, verifiable, clear information is mandatory.

* Lamudi listings carry a standard footer referencing NOM-247-SE-2021 and the portal's PROFECO adhesion contract registration number (e.g., Contract 924-2025 on Lamudi). Platforms must replicate or reference this disclosure where required.

* There is no national feed standard. Each portal has its own proprietary API and XML/JSON feed format. Platforms must build per-portal integrations.

* QuintoAndar (Inmuebles24 + Vivanuncios) blocked EasyBroker CRM feeds in 2023 and offers its own free CRM — platforms should monitor ongoing access restrictions.

* Duplicate listings are endemic — the same property often appears on multiple portals simultaneously under different agencies (legal under non-exclusive contracts).

* Private individuals may list on most portals without AMPI membership or NOM-247 compliance (NOM-247 applies to commercial service providers only).

IVA / VAT ON PORTAL FEES: Portal listing fees are subject to 16% IVA. Platforms and agencies must obtain valid CFDI (electronic invoice) from portals for tax deduction purposes.

sections.title: NOM-247-SE-2021 — Consumer Protection Standard
sections.contentHtml:
The Norma Oficial Mexicana NOM-247-SE-2021 (Commercial Practices — Requirements for Commercial Information and Advertising of Real Estate Intended for Housing, and Minimum Elements that Related Contracts Must Contain) entered into force on September 19, 2022. It is mandatory for all commercial service providers marketing residential property in Mexico, including agents, brokers, developers, builders, and — critically for marketplace platforms — any entity involved in the advice or sale of residential real estate to the public.

SCOPE: NOM-247 applies to: developers, builders, promoters, intermediaries, brokers, and any natural or legal person involved in the commercial advice or sale of residential real estate. It does NOT apply to private individuals selling their own property directly.

Advertising and Listing Display Requirements

* All advertising must be truthful, verifiable, clear, and not misleading or deceptive.

* The sale price must be displayed clearly, including all costs and taxes the consumer must pay.

* Property features (size, construction year, location, amenities) must match what is actually delivered.

* Availability must be kept current — listings for sold or unavailable properties must be removed promptly.

* All portal and digital advertising is covered — NOM-247 explicitly includes digital media.

* Pre-sale listings must clearly identify the property as pre-sale (preventa) and state the expected delivery date.

PROFECO Contract Registration

One of NOM-247's most significant requirements for marketplace platforms is the mandatory PROFECO adhesion contract registration. Any service provider offering residential real estate must register their standard client contracts with PROFECO before commercialisation. This includes listing agreements, mediation contracts, and purchase agreements.

* Two approved contract models exist: the PROFECO version and the AMPI version.

* PROFECO's jurisdiction means faster conciliation if a consumer dispute arises.

* Platforms that use standardised agency onboarding contracts must ensure those contracts are PROFECO-registered.

* Over 50 individual compliance points exist within NOM-247 — platforms should conduct a full NOM-247 gap analysis.

Consumer Rights Under NOM-247

Every consumer in a residential real estate transaction in Mexico holds the following rights under NOM-247:

* Receive truthful, clear, and updated information and advertising, including via digital media.

* Be informed of all activity and updated on the status of their property transaction.

* Have the agent act in their best interests throughout the transaction (fiduciary duty).

* Have all offers promptly communicated.

* Have all commission rates, fees, and costs explicitly stated in the contract.

* Receive proof of any advance payment or deposit made.

* Select the notary public who will formalize the purchase.

* Be protected by PROFECO conciliation in case of dispute.

Penalties for Non-Compliance

* Maximum PROFECO fine: MXN $5,116,688 (approximately USD $250,000 at current exchange rates).

* PROFECO can also suspend commercial activities for serious violations.

* Penalties apply per violation — multiple non-compliant listings can result in cumulative fines.

sections.title: Foreign Ownership Rules and the Fideicomiso
sections.contentHtml:
Mexico's Constitutional restriction on foreign property ownership in coastal and border areas is one of the most important compliance considerations for any marketplace platform serving international buyers. All platforms must correctly identify whether a property falls inside or outside the Restricted Zone and surface the appropriate ownership structure accordingly.

The Restricted Zone

ZoneDefinition
Foreign Ownership RuleRestricted Zone — coastal
All land within 50 kilometers of any coastlineDirect foreign ownership PROHIBITED. Must use fideicomiso or Mexican corporation.
Restricted Zone — borderAll land within 100 kilometers of any international border
Direct foreign ownership PROHIBITED. Must use fideicomiso or Mexican corporation.Interior Mexico (outside restricted zone)
All other areas: Mexico City, Guadalajara, Monterrey, Querétaro, and most of the interiorDirect foreign ownership PERMITTED. Foreigner registers directly in their name in the Public Property Registry.
CRITICAL: The restricted zone applies equally to ALL foreign nationalities — there is no preferential treatment for U.S., Canadian, EU, or any other passport. All foreign buyers face identical constitutional restrictions. 

The Fideicomiso — Bank Trust Structure

The fideicomiso is a legally recognized bank trust that allows foreign buyers to hold property rights in Mexico's Restricted Zone. Under the fideicomiso structure, a Mexican bank holds legal title as trustee while the foreign buyer is the trust beneficiary with full practical ownership rights. The structure is legally sound, widely used, and specifically designed by Mexican law to facilitate international investment.

FeatureDetail
Trust duration50 years — renewable indefinitely
Foreign buyer's rightsFull: sell, lease, renovate, mortgage, bequeath — all require only the buyer's written instruction to the bank
Bank's roleTrustee — holds legal title only. Cannot act without buyer's written approval.
SRE permitRequired from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (SRE). Typically approved within 5 business days.
Setup costUSD $2,000–$3,000 (one-time). Includes bank administrative work and legal filings.
Annual feeUSD $550–$1,000 per year to the trustee bank for ongoing trust management.
Setup timeline45–60 days average from SRE permit approval to closing.
InheritanceBeneficiaries named in the trust document. Property passes without Mexican probate.
Commercial properties (non-residential)Foreigners may hold directly through a 100% foreign-owned Mexican corporation in the restricted zone for commercial purposes.

Outside the Restricted Zone — Direct Ownership

Foreigners purchasing property outside the restricted zone — including Mexico City, Guadalajara, Monterrey, Querétaro, and most interior cities — may hold title directly in their own name, registered in the Public Property Registry. This is called dominio pleno (freehold). As of early 2026, this form of ownership is fully available to all foreign nationals with no additional structures required.

Ejido Land — Critical Risk

Ejido land is communal agricultural land under a special constitutional classification. Ejido land cannot be directly purchased by foreigners and in many cases cannot be transacted at all without a formal conversion process. Platforms must ensure listings do not display ejido-classified land without clearly flagging the classification and the legal complexity. Purchasing ejido land without proper legal conversion is a common and serious mistake for foreign buyers.

PLATFORM OBLIGATION: Any listing involving ejido-classified land should carry a clear disclosure label. Platforms should not display ejido land as straightforwardly purchasable without surfacing the legal risk prominently.

________________

sections.title: Standard Transaction Process
sections.contentHtml:
The standard Mexican property purchase follows a defined sequence involving the notario público as the central legal authority. Unlike in the U.S., the notario in Mexico is a government-appointed official — not a private attorney — who has binding responsibility for the legality and tax compliance of the transaction.

StepAction
PartyKey Compliance Point
1Obtain RFC (fiscal ID)
BuyerRequired before any contract or bank account. Foreign buyers obtain at SAT office or via power of attorney.
2Property search — portals or regional MLS
BuyerVerify agent's AMPI membership and NOM-247 contract registration if applicable. Check listing is not ejido land.
3Engage a notario público and independent lawyer
BuyerNotario conducts due diligence: title search, lien check, tax status, seller authority. Hire independent legal counsel (separate from notario and seller's agent).
4Restricted zone check
Buyer / LawyerConfirm property coordinates with SRE if near coast or border. Determine if fideicomiso is required.
5SRE fideicomiso permit (if in restricted zone)
Buyer / BankApply through Mexican bank. SRE approval typically within 5 business days.
6Sign purchase agreement / promissory contract
Buyer + SellerDeposit paid (typically 10–30%). Document must comply with NOM-247 contract requirements.
7AML / LFPIORPI due diligence
All partiesKYC required. Proof of funds source. Beneficial owner identification. Cash restrictions apply.
8Pay ISAI (acquisition tax)
BuyerRate varies 2–5% by state. Paid to notario before or at closing.
9Closing — sign Escritura Pública before notario
Buyer + Seller + NotarioNotario verifies all documents, taxes paid. Deed executed. Balance paid (typically by wire transfer).
10Register in Public Property Registry (RPPyC)
Buyer / NotarioRegistration completes ownership transfer. Notario typically handles this.
11Predial (property tax) obligations begin
Buyer (new owner)Annual predial billed by municipality. Early payment (Jan–Feb) = up to 25% discount.

The Notario Público — Central Role

The notario público (Notary Public) in Mexico is a government-appointed professional with legal training who serves simultaneously as a legal authority and a government agent. The notario's role is far broader than in common-law countries: they verify title, confirm all taxes are paid, validate the seller's authority, assess AML compliance, and officially register the deed. All real estate transactions in Mexico must be executed before a notario.

* The buyer has the right under NOM-247 to select the notario who will formalize the purchase.

* Notario fees: typically 0.75–1.5% of property value. Fees are regulated by state aranceles (fee schedules).

* Notario cannot proceed without proof of ISAI payment, clear title, and satisfied LFPIORPI requirements.

LFPIORPI NOTARIO THRESHOLD — July 2025 Reform (Previously Missing): The July 2025 reform reduced the notario's LFPIORPI reporting threshold for real property transfers from 16,000 UMAs to 8,000 UMAs (approximately MXN $904,000 at 2025 UMA values). Any property transfer at or above this value now triggers mandatory notario due diligence and SAT reporting. Platforms displaying property values should flag listings at or above this threshold in any compliance workflow or transaction management tool.

sections.title: Tax Reference — Property Transactions
sections.contentHtml:
Mexico's property tax system is primarily administered at the state and municipal level. Federal taxes apply to income (ISR) and sales tax (IVA). The key taxes for marketplace platforms to understand are the acquisition tax (ISAI), annual property tax (Predial), and capital gains tax (ISR on gains).

ISAI — Property Acquisition Tax

The Impuesto Sobre Adquisición de Inmuebles (ISAI) is a one-time tax on property transfer, payable by the buyer at closing. Rates are set by each state and municipality and vary significantly across Mexico.

State / MarketISAI Rate
Calculation BasisNotes
Quintana Roo (Cancún, Tulum, Playa del Carmen)2%
Purchase priceOne of the lowest rates. Favourable for Riviera Maya buyers.
Baja California Sur (Los Cabos)3%
Purchase pricePopular foreign buyer market.
Mexico City (CDMX)4–8% (progressive)
Purchase price (higher brackets apply to higher values)Progressive scale; total closing costs 6–9% for most CDMX transactions.
Jalisco (Guadalajara)2–4%
Purchase price or cadastral value (higher)Verify current rate with state tax authority.
Nuevo León (Monterrey)2–3%
Purchase priceCompetitive industrial market.
National range2–5%
VariesForeigners pay same rate as Mexican nationals — no surcharge.
IMPORTANT: Residential property purchases are typically exempt from IVA (VAT) when used as a primary dwelling (casa habitación). However, new-build purchases may have VAT components on construction services depending on how the developer structures invoices. Verify on a per-transaction basis. 

Predial — Annual Municipal Property Tax

* Calculated as a percentage of the property's cadastral value (the municipal assessed value, typically 30–70% below market value).

* National rate range: approximately 0.1–0.45% of cadastral value per year — very low compared to U.S. or European standards.

* Paid annually, typically by 31 March. Monthly payment options available in some municipalities.

* Early payment discount: 5–25% available for payments made in January–February (varies by municipality).

* Predial is assessed against the owner of record as of 1 January of the tax year.

* Unpaid Predial is a lien against the property — platforms advising buyers should flag due diligence on Predial arrears.

ISR — Capital Gains Tax on Sale

Seller TypeRate
CalculationExemptions
Mexican resident individual1.92–35% (progressive)
Applied to NET gain after allowable deductionsPrimary residence exemption available — see below
Non-resident individual (default)25% flat
Applied to GROSS sale proceedsNo deductions under default method
Non-resident individual (elective)35% on net gain
Applied to NET gain if seller elects to file Mexican ISR returnAllows cost deductions; may be lower than 25% of gross for high-cost properties
Mexican corporation30% corporate ISR
Applied to net gain under accrual accountingStandard corporate deductions apply
* Primary residence exemption (residents only): exempt from ISR if property was used as primary home, seller has tax residency in Mexico, sale is not within 5 years of a previous exemption, and the sale does not exceed certain value thresholds. A notario certification of primary residence is required. 

* Deductible costs that reduce the taxable gain: original purchase price + ISAI + notary fees + agent commission at purchase + documented improvement costs.

* ISR at sale is calculated and withheld by the notario at closing.

IVA / VAT on Rental Income

* Short-term rentals (under 28 days, including Airbnb/VRBO-type): subject to 16% IVA on gross rental income.

* Long-term residential rentals: exempt from IVA.

* ISH (Impuesto Sobre Hospedaje — Lodging Tax): 5% in Quintana Roo and other tourist states. Applied to short-term rental income. Rate varies by state.

* Rental income (ISR): Mexican residents pay progressive rate on net rental profit. Non-residents: 25% flat on gross unless electing to file a Mexican return.

* Monthly ISR and IVA returns due by the 17th of the following month via SAT's online portal.

Total Closing Costs — Buyer Estimate

Cost ItemTypical Range
Paid ByNotes
ISAI (acquisition tax)2–5% of purchase price
BuyerRate set by state. Most significant single cost.
Notario fees0.75–1.5% of property value
Buyer (typically)Regulated by state fee schedules.
Public Registry fees0.2–0.5%
BuyerRegistration in RPPyC.
Appraisal (avalúo)MXN 3,000–10,000
BuyerRequired by banks; recommended for all transactions.
Title search / certificatesMXN 1,000–5,000
BuyerLien search, tax status certificate.
Fideicomiso setup (restricted zone)USD $2,000–$3,000
Buyer (foreigner)One-time fee. Bank administrative + legal filings.
SRE permit (restricted zone)Fixed government fee
Buyer (foreigner)Nominal cost.
Independent lawyerUSD $1,000–$3,000+
BuyerHighly recommended for foreign buyers.
Agent commission (seller's agent)4–6% + 16% IVA
SellerPaid by seller. Buyer agent less common.
Total buyer closing costs (est.)5–8% of purchase price
BuyerExcludes fideicomiso costs. Higher in CDMX due to progressive ISAI.

________________

sections.title: Nuevo León Civil Code Reform — Pre-Sale Rules (Effective March 17, 2026)
sections.contentHtml:
On January 16, 2026, the Congress of the State of Nuevo León published Decree Number 178 in the Official Gazette (Periódico Oficial del Estado), amending the Civil Code of Nuevo León to expressly regulate real estate pre-sale offers. The reform enters into force on March 17, 2026 — today — making this the most time-sensitive compliance item in this document for marketplace platforms operating in Nuevo León or distributing listings from the Monterrey metropolitan area.

EFFECTIVE TODAY — MARCH 17 2026: Any pre-sale listing or pre-sale offer received or published in Nuevo León on or after March 17, 2026 is subject to the new Civil Code provisions. Platforms must update their listing intake workflows, pre-sale disclosure logic, and developer onboarding immediately.

Legal Definition of Pre-Sale — Now Codified

The reform introduces Article 1720 Bis and six subsidiary articles. For the first time in Nuevo León, a legal definition of real estate pre-sale is codified. A pre-sale offer is defined as an offer to purchase a real estate property with respect to which, at the time of the offer, the owner of the future real estate is not yet legally able to transfer the property because the property is still under development.

Construction License Requirement

Article 1720 Bis 2 imposes the most significant obligation for developers and platforms. Only the holder of a valid, in-force construction license (licencia de construcción) may receive pre-sale offers from buyers. The key rules are:

* Mandatory pre-condition: a valid construction license must exist before any pre-sale offer can be made or received.

* No license = no pre-sale: if no valid construction license exists, no pre-sale offers may be accepted.

* Assignment allowed: the license holder may assign their rights to receive pre-sale offers to another party, BUT the assignment must be made in writing and ratified before a notary public.

* Sales authorization vs construction license: a developer may offer pre-sales even if the formal sales authorization (autorización de ventas) required by Nuevo León's Urban Development Law has not yet been issued — but if that authorization is subsequently denied, the developer must return all amounts received plus 9% annual interest within 60 calendar days.

Offer Acceptance Timeline

* Once a pre-sale offer is received, the developer has a maximum of 3 months to notify acceptance.

* If 3 months pass without express acceptance, the offer is legally deemed rejected.

* Acceptance of the pre-sale offer does NOT constitute a definitive sale — it is a binding preliminary commitment, not a completed transfer.

* Delivery of any deposit or advance payment by the buyer constitutes automatic acceptance of the offer by the developer.

Developer Disclosure Obligations

Once a pre-sale has been accepted, the developer (recipient of the offer) is obligated to promptly inform the buyer (offeror) if either of the following occurs:

* Expiration or lapse of the construction license

* Denial of the sales authorization by the relevant urban development authority

Upon either event, the buyer has the right to revoke the pre-sale offer. The developer must then return ALL amounts received, plus interest at a minimum annual rate of 9%, within 60 calendar days of the revocation. This right cannot be waived in advance by the buyer.

Penalties and Enforcement

* Penalties for non-compliance (e.g. late delivery, default on obligations) may be agreed by both parties in the pre-sale document.

* Agreed penalties are enforceable without the need for a prior judicial declaration — a significant departure from prior law that required court proceedings.

* In case of non-payment of agreed penalties or refunds, obligations may be enforced directly.

Minimum Content of Pre-Sale Document

The reform establishes minimum content requirements for the document in which the pre-sale is recorded. Platforms displaying pre-construction listings or facilitating pre-sale transactions must ensure developer-supplied documents contain these elements:

* Identification of both parties (offeror/buyer and recipient/developer)

* Full property description including location, dimensions, and intended use

* Reference to the construction license number and issuing authority

* Total price and payment schedule

* Expected completion and delivery date

* Terms for return of deposits if construction license expires or sales authorization is denied

* Agreed penalties for non-compliance by either party

Platform Compliance Obligations for Nuevo León Pre-Sale Listings

ObligationDetail
JSON FieldConstruction license verification
Before publishing any new-build or pre-construction listing in Nuevo León, verify that developer holds a valid construction license. Display license number on listing.nl_construction_license_number, nl_construction_license_valid
Pre-sale disclosure labelLabel all pre-construction listings in Nuevo León as 'pre-sale' (preventa) with expected delivery date.
nl_presale_listing, nl_expected_delivery_dateAssignment verification
If developer is not the construction license holder, verify notarized written assignment exists.nl_license_assignment_notarized
Deposit protection disclosureInform buyers that deposits are refundable plus 9% annual interest if license expires or sales authorization denied.
nl_deposit_protection_disclosed3-month acceptance window
Flag listings where offer has not been accepted within 3 months as requiring status review.nl_offer_acceptance_deadline
Developer disclosure trackingTrack whether developer has notified buyers of any license expiry or sales authorization denial.
nl_disclosure_compliantNOM-247 INTERACTION: The Nuevo León reform operates alongside NOM-247-SE-2021. Both apply to pre-sale marketing in Nuevo León. NOM-247 governs federal advertising standards; the Civil Code reform governs the contractual and legal structure of the pre-sale itself. Platforms must comply with both.

________________

sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering — LFPIORPI (July 2025 Reform)
sections.contentHtml:
The Ley Federal para la Prevención e Identificación de Operaciones con Recursos de Procedencia Ilícita (LFPIORPI) is Mexico's primary anti-money laundering law. Real estate transactions have always been classified as 'vulnerable activities' under LFPIORPI. The July 16, 2025 reform — published in the Diario Oficial de la Federación — significantly expanded obligations, raised thresholds, added new vulnerable activities, and increased penalties.

Key 2025 Reform Changes

ChangeDetail
Effective DateReal estate development as vulnerable activity
Receipt of funds for real estate development projects for sale or rental is now explicitly a reportable vulnerable activity if the threshold of 8,025 UMAs (~MXN $907,948 in 2025) is exceeded.July 17, 2025
Beneficial owner threshold reduced + effective control addedOwnership interest threshold reduced from 25% to 20%. Critically, the reform also adds an 'effective control' clause — individuals who exercise effective control over a legal entity must be identified even if their formal ownership stake is below 20%. This broadens the definition significantly beyond a simple percentage test.
July 17, 2025Cash restriction expanded — includes bank-routed payments
Amended Article 32 explicitly prohibits settling real estate obligations using coins, banknotes (national or foreign currency), or precious metals — whether handed over directly OR processed through a financial institution. The restriction is not limited to physical cash exchanges; payments routed through a bank in cash form are equally prohibited above threshold.July 17, 2025
Internal policies manualAll obligated entities must prepare and maintain an internal AML policies and procedures manual.
July 17, 2025 (implementation rules pending)Automated monitoring systems
Implementation of automated transaction monitoring mechanisms required for continuous oversight.Implementation rules pending (up to 12 months)
Annual auditsInternal or external audits required annually depending on risk level.
Implementation rules pendingFideicomiso / trust obligations
Trusts are explicitly subject to all LFPIORPI obligations, including beneficial owner identification.July 17, 2025
Increased penaltiesFines up to 65,000 times the daily UMA value, OR 100% of the transaction value — whichever is greater.
July 17, 2025Voluntary compliance benefit
One-time benefit: SHCP may waive penalties for voluntary regularization before an official verification begins.July 17, 2025

Real Estate Marketplace Platform Obligations

Marketplace platforms that facilitate real estate transactions or provide matching services between buyers and developers may be classified as 'obligated subjects' under LFPIORPI. Platforms should seek formal legal advice on whether their specific business model triggers LFPIORPI obligations. The following are the standard obligations for any entity classified as an obligated subject:

* KYC (Know Your Customer): identify and verify clients and beneficial owners before executing transactions.

* Beneficial owner identification: 20% formal ownership OR effective control — whichever is triggered first. Individuals exercising effective control over an entity must be identified even if their formal stake falls below 20%. The 20% is a floor, not an absolute ceiling.

* Cash restrictions (Article 32, amended July 2025): prohibited above UMA threshold whether physical cash OR payments routed through a financial institution in cash form. Wire transfer (SPEI) is the required method for qualifying transactions.

* SAT reporting: file activity reports with SAT's Vulnerable Activities portal (portaldeobligaciones.sat.gob.mx).

* Record retention: maintain all client identification and transaction records for at least 10 years.

* Designate compliance officer: register with SHCP.

* Internal policies manual: document all AML procedures.

* Automated monitoring: implement transaction monitoring systems.

* Annual audit: internal or external depending on risk classification.

IMPORTANT: Implementation rules (Reglas Generales) for some new 2025 reform obligations are still being issued by SHCP and SAT. Platforms must monitor the Diario Oficial de la Federación for these secondary regulations as they are published.

________________

sections.title: Data Privacy — LFPDPPP
sections.contentHtml:
Mexico's data protection framework is governed by the Ley Federal de Protección de Datos Personales en Posesión de los Particulares (LFPDPPP), enacted in 2010, and its Reglamento (implementing regulations) published in 2011. While modelled on OECD principles, Mexico's data protection law is less prescriptive than the EU's GDPR — there is no mandatory data protection officer requirement or formal certification process. The supervising authority is the INAI.

INAI — Data Protection Authority

The Instituto Nacional de Transparencia, Acceso a la Información y Protección de Datos Personales (INAI) is Mexico's independent data protection authority. INAI supervises compliance with LFPDPPP and can impose fines and corrective measures on private-sector entities. Real estate marketplace platforms collecting user data are classified as responsible parties (responsables) under LFPDPPP.

* Website: inai.org.mx

* Maximum fine: MXN $322,120–MXN $96,636,000 (fine range set by LFPDPPP Art. 64, adjusted by index)

Key Platform Obligations

* Aviso de Privacidad (Privacy Notice): mandatory — must be provided before collecting personal data. Must describe purposes of processing, data transfers, and subject rights.

* Consent: required for sensitive data processing and for secondary uses of data not originally disclosed.

* Data subject rights (ARCO): all data subjects have the right to Access, Rectification, Cancellation, and Objection (ARCO) — platforms must implement mechanisms to honor these.

* Data security: reasonable technical and organisational measures to protect personal data.

* Data transfers: international data transfers to countries without adequate protection require express consent or contractual clauses.

* Sensitive data: geographic location, financial information, biometrics — require express consent and heightened protection.

GDPR vs LFPDPPP: Mexico's LFPDPPP is opt-out based (not opt-in) for most processing. It is less stringent than GDPR. However, for platforms serving users in Mexico AND Europe, the stricter GDPR standard should be applied globally to avoid compliance gaps.

Email and Digital Marketing

* Mexico's LFPDPPP allows opt-out for commercial communications — not prior opt-in as required in the EU.

* All commercial emails must clearly identify the sender and include an opt-out/unsubscribe mechanism.

* The Aviso de Privacidad must disclose whether personal data will be used for marketing purposes.

* If data was collected for a different primary purpose, express consent is required before using it for marketing.

sections.title: Regional Market Compliance Notes
sections.contentHtml:
Mexico City (CDMX)

* Outside the restricted zone — direct title available to all foreign buyers.

* ISAI is progressive: can reach 4–8% for higher-value properties.

* No formal AMPI MLS — entirely portal-driven.

* Portal Inmuebles24 is dominant. Propiedades.com is strong in residential.

* CDMX is Mexico's most regulated rental market — verify current rent control and housing protections under the Ley de Vivienda for long-term rental listings.

Riviera Maya — Cancún, Playa del Carmen, Tulum (Quintana Roo)

* Entire market is within the 50km coastal restricted zone — fideicomiso required for all foreign buyers.

* ISAI: 2% (among the lowest in Mexico).

* ISH (lodging tax): 5% on short-term rental income in Quintana Roo.

* IVA on short-term rentals: 16% on gross income.

* Tulum: significant ejido land risk — buyers and platforms must verify land classification with National Agrarian Registry (RAN) before listing or transacting.

* New hotel zone developments (e.g., Tulum hotel zone) may require environmental impact permits and SEMARNAT approvals.

Los Cabos (Baja California Sur)

* Entire market is within the 50km coastal restricted zone — fideicomiso required for all foreign buyers.

* ISAI: 3%.

* Highest average sale prices in Mexico for foreign buyers after some CDMX neighbourhoods.

* Los Cabos MLS is the dominant data platform — AMPI Los Cabos affiliated.

* Strong U.S. and Canadian buyer base — listings should ideally be bilingual.

Puerto Vallarta / Riviera Nayarit

* Coastal restricted zone applies — fideicomiso required for foreign residential buyers.

* Vallarta-Nayarit MLS is the most established regional MLS in Mexico, operating since 1988.

* Dual-state market: Jalisco (Vallarta) and Nayarit — different state tax rates may apply depending on property side.

* AMPI Vallarta, AMPI Riviera Nayarit, and AMPI Compostela all operate in this market.

Monterrey / Nuevo León

* Major industrial and business city. Not in the restricted zone.

* Direct foreign ownership permitted.

* ISAI: 2–3%.

* No regional MLS — portal-driven.

* Strong nearshoring investment demand driving commercial and residential activity in 2025–2026.
